title: pH testing for archival papermaking
subtitle: Why acidity destroys paper and how to check and correct your vat water
excerpt: Paper made in acidic water turns brittle and yellow within decades. The cause is residual acid in the cellulose fibres, which slowly breaks down the fibre chains in a process called acid hydrolysis. Testing the pH of your vat water and pulp before forming sheets costs thirty seconds and can make the difference between a sheet that lasts a hundred years and one that crumbles in twenty.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Papermaking: The History and Technique of an Ancient Craft (1943), Hunter discusses acid degradation in paper and the importance of neutral-to-alkaline formation conditions. The chemistry of acid hydrolysis in cellulose is well established and documented in twentieth-century conservation science.

glossaryTerms.definition: The chemical breakdown of cellulose fibre chains by residual acid within the paper. Produces shorter and shorter fibre chains over time, leading to a brittle, yellowed, and eventually friable sheet. The primary cause of historic paper deterioration.

A small excess of alkaline material (typically calcium carbonate) added to paper during manufacture to neutralise acids that may form over time or migrate from adjacent materials. Archival papers have a pH of 7.5 to 9.
Paper made and stored at or above pH 7. Neutral or slightly alkaline conditions prevent acid hydrolysis; archival conservation standards require pH 7 or above for permanent paper.

content.text: is the enemy of paper permanence. It works slowly and invisibly: a sheet made in acidic water looks fine for years, then begins to yellow at the edges, then becomes brittle, then finally crumbles. The acid in old newspaper is a visible example, newsprint is made without pH correction and degrades within a generation. For archival work,

items.name: pH testing strips, range 5 to 9 (or a wider range)
items.qty: a small packet, lasts for many sessions
items.substitutions: A digital pH meter (more accurate; recommended if you make paper regularly)

content.text: Dip a pH testing strip into the vat water for 1 to 2 seconds. Remove it and compare the colour change to the chart on the packet within 30 seconds, do not wait longer as the colour continues to develop.
Aim for pH 7 to 8.5 for archival work. pH 6.5 is acceptable for decorative papers that do not need long-term permanence. Below pH 6, correct the water before forming sheets.

To raise pH: dissolve a small amount of calcium carbonate (chalk) or bicarbonate of soda in the vat water. Add a pinch at a time, stir, and re-test. Each addition raises the pH by a small increment; go slowly.

For truly archival work, add a small amount of calcium carbonate directly to the pulp during beating. This builds an alkaline reserve into the paper itself, a buffer that will neutralise any acids that form later from atmospheric pollution or migration from adjacent acidic materials. The standard quantity is 2% calcium carbonate by dry weight of pulp.

Test the vat at the start of each session. Tap water pH varies by season and by area (softwater regions tend to be slightly acidic). If you are using plant-fibre pulps that have been cooked in soda ash, the pulp itself may be alkaline and will raise the vat pH, test before correcting either direction.

items.symptom: pH reads 9 or above, too alkaline
items.cause: Too much calcium carbonate or soda ash added; or plant fibre not adequately rinsed after alkaline cooking.
items.fix: Add a small amount of white vinegar (dilute acetic acid) drop by drop to bring the pH back toward neutral. Re-test after each addition.

items.cause: The strips are old or have been exposed to moisture in storage.
items.fix: Replace the strips. Store in a sealed container. A digital pH meter is more reliable for regular use.
